---
title: "Moment Generating and Characteristic Functions"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

DENS   = "#2b3a67"     # densities / data
TRANSF = "#c1121f"     # the transform M(t) or phi(t)
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / exact values
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second of the two articles that close
> Movement 4 of the Probability Foundations track — the **tail-and-limit** machinery that
> the downstream tracks consume. Its sibling, [PROB-19 *Chebyshev's Inequality and the
> Concentration Idea*], bounded how much probability can sit far from the mean using only
> the first two moments; this article builds the object that packages **all** the moments
> at once and turns sums of independent variables into products. It assumes [PROB-05
> *Expectation*] (the expectation operator and the law of the unconscious statistician),
> [PROB-06 *Variance*] and [PROB-07 *Covariance*] (the second moment), and [PROB-08
> *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*] (the factoring of expectations of products that
> makes the convolution property work). To compute the transforms of named distributions
> we lean on the catalog: [PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial, Negative-Binomial*], [PROB-12
> *The Poisson Distribution*], [PROB-13 *The Normal Distribution*], and [PROB-14 *Gamma,
> Exponential, Beta*]. Complex exponentials, needed for the characteristic function, are
> introduced from scratch here — nothing above the Calc-1 floor is assumed. It **sets
> up** [PROB-21 *Modes of Convergence*], where the characteristic function becomes the
> tool that defines and detects convergence *in distribution*, and it hands the
> **central-limit machinery** to [INFR-A2], which owns the CLT proof itself and only
> borrows the transform built here.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A distribution is a complicated object — a whole function, the density or mass. This
article builds a single auxiliary function that stands in for it: the **moment generating
function** $M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]$. Two facts make it indispensable. First, differentiating
$M_X$ at $t=0$ pours out the moments one at a time — $M_X'(0)=E[X]$, $M_X''(0)=E[X^2]$,
and so on — so one function encodes the entire moment sequence. Second, the MGF of a sum
of *independent* variables is the *product* of their MGFs, which converts the painful
convolution integral for the distribution of a sum into ordinary multiplication. Together
with a uniqueness theorem — equal MGFs mean equal distributions — these turn questions
like "what is the distribution of a sum of independent normals?" into one-line algebra.
The single claim this article defends is that **the MGF is the right bookkeeping device
for moments and sums, exactly when it exists** — and that when heavy tails make it blow
up, replacing $e^{tX}$ with $e^{itX}$ gives the **characteristic function**, which always
exists and inherits every good property at the cost of carrying an imaginary unit.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Attach to a random variable $X$ the function $M_X(t) = E\!\left[e^{tX}\right]$. Because
$e^{tX}$ expands as $1 + tX + \tfrac{t^2}{2}X^2 + \cdots$, the function $M_X$ carries
**every moment of $X$** in its derivatives at $t=0$: the $k$-th derivative
$M_X^{(k)}(0)$ equals $E[X^k]$. And because the expectation of a product of independent
quantities factors, the MGF of a **sum of independent variables is the product of their
MGFs**. Moments-by-differentiation and sums-by-multiplication are the two reasons this
transform exists. When the tails are too heavy for $E[e^{tX}]$ to be finite, swap the
real exponential for the complex one: the **characteristic function**
$\varphi_X(t) = E\!\left[e^{itX}\right]$ always exists, because $|e^{itX}| = 1$, and keeps
all the same powers.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The transform $M_X(t)=E[e^{tX}]$ will look, at first,
like an unmotivated thing to compute — why would anyone average an exponential of a
random variable? But by the end you should see it as inevitable. We are looking for a
device that (a) repackages the infinite list of moments into one manageable object and
(b) behaves well under the one operation we constantly perform on random variables —
adding independent ones together. The exponential is the *unique* elementary function
that does both at once, because its Taylor series lays out the powers $X^k$ for property
(a) and its functional equation $e^{a+b}=e^a e^b$ delivers property (b). Hold those two
demands in mind and the rest is consequence.

# Why we are here: two problems that needed one tool {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **is there a single function that
encodes all of a distribution's moments and turns sums of independents into products?**
Every derivation below answers a piece of that question.

## The problem of the sum {#sec-why-sum}

The single most common thing we do to random variables is **add independent ones up**.
The total of $n$ daily returns, the sum of waiting times across a queue, the count of
successes across many trials, the aggregate of many small measurement errors — all are
sums $S_n = X_1 + \cdots + X_n$. So the recurring question is: *given the distributions of
the pieces, what is the distribution of the sum?*

For two independent continuous variables $X$ and $Y$ with densities $f_X$ and $f_Y$, the
density of $S = X+Y$ is the **convolution**

$$
f_S(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, f_Y(s-x)\, dx .
$$ {#eq-convolution}

This is built carefully in the joint-distribution work of [PROB-07]; here we only need to
register how *unpleasant* it is. To add three variables you convolve twice; to add $n$ you
convolve $n-1$ times, each a fresh integral over the whole line. The integrand changes
every time and rarely simplifies. By hand, the convolution route to "what is the
distribution of a sum of ten gammas?" is hopeless.

Yet the answers are often astonishingly clean — a sum of independent normals is normal, a
sum of independent Poissons is Poisson, a sum of independent gammas with a common rate is
gamma. The convolution integral *buries* that simplicity under calculus. The historical
demand was for a device that exposes it. That device exists precisely because there is a
transform under which **convolution becomes multiplication.**

## The problem of the moments {#sec-why-moments}

The second recurring need is for the **moments** of a distribution: the mean
$E[X]$, the variance built from $E[X^2]$, the skewness from $E[X^3]$, the kurtosis from
$E[X^4]$. Each is an expectation $E[X^k] = \int x^k f(x)\,dx$ (or the analogous sum in the
discrete case), and each requires its own integral. For a distribution with a complicated
density, computing the fourth moment is a separate, often grueling, integration from the
second.

It would be a great economy to compute *one* object from which **every** moment can be
read off by differentiation — turning a sequence of integrals into a sequence of
derivatives, which are mechanical. That object is the moment generating function, and the
name is a literal description of what it does.

## One tool, two jobs — and the historical thread {#sec-why-history}

These two needs are met by the same function because of one algebraic coincidence: the
exponential's Taylor series solves the moment problem (its powers are exactly $X^k$) and
its product rule solves the sum problem ($e^{tX}e^{tY}=e^{t(X+Y)}$). Pierre-Simon Laplace,
around the turn of the nineteenth century, was already using what we would now call
generating functions and the closely related Laplace transform to attack sums of random
quantities and the first central-limit phenomena. The fully general **characteristic
function** — the version with the imaginary unit, which sidesteps the existence problems
that plague the MGF — was placed at the center of probability by Paul Lévy in the 1920s
and 1930s, who showed that convergence of characteristic functions is *exactly*
convergence in distribution. That last fact is what makes these transforms the engine of
the central limit theorem, the destination [INFR-A2] builds toward and the reason this
article is a prerequisite for it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just work with the density directly? What does the transform buy you?"* Two
things, and you should name both. **(1) Sums become tractable.** The distribution of a sum
of independents is a convolution of densities — an integral that compounds with each added
term. Under the MGF (or characteristic function) it is a *product*, which compounds
trivially: the MGF of a sum of $n$ i.i.d. pieces is just the single MGF raised to the
$n$-th power. **(2) Moments become derivatives.** Instead of a separate integral for each
$E[X^k]$, you differentiate one function repeatedly at zero. The transform trades a hard
integral (convolution) and many hard integrals (the moments) for easy multiplication and
easy differentiation. That is the entire value proposition.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formal definition, see what $M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]$ *is*. Fix the random variable
$X$. For each fixed number $t$, the quantity $e^{tX}$ is a new random variable — $X$ run
through the function $u\mapsto e^{tu}$ — and we average it. Sweep $t$ across the real line
and you trace out a function of $t$. That function is $M_X$.

The factor $e^{tX}$ is a **tilt**. When $t>0$, $e^{tX}$ is large where $X$ is large, so
the average $E[e^{tX}]$ weights the right tail heavily; when $t<0$ it leans on the left
tail. Because $e^{tu}$ grows faster than any power of $u$, the average can be infinite if
the distribution puts too much mass far out — which is exactly the existence problem we
will meet in @sec-existence, and exactly why heavy-tailed distributions break the MGF. The
derivative of this tilt at $t=0$ measures how sharply the average responds to a tiny tilt,
and that sensitivity is the mean; the second derivative is the next-order sensitivity, and
that is $E[X^2]$. @fig-tilt makes the tilt visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-tilt
#| fig-cap: 'The MGF as a tilted average. The grey curve is a standard normal density $f(x)$. Multiplying by $e^{tx}$ (here $t=0.8$) tilts it: the dashed red curve is the *unnormalized* tilted weight $e^{tx} f(x)$, which leans toward the right tail. The MGF $M(t)=\int e^{tx} f(x)\,dx$ is the total area under that tilted curve — a single number for each $t$. As $t$ grows the tilt is more violent, and if the original density did not decay fast enough the area would be infinite.'
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 400)
f = np.exp(-x**2 / 2) / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi)
t = 0.8
tilt = np.exp(t * x) * f

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, f, color=GREY, label=r"density $f(x)$ (standard normal)")
ax.plot(x, tilt, color=TRANSF, ls="--",
        label=fr"tilted weight $e^{{{t}x}}\,f(x)$")
ax.fill_between(x, tilt, color=TRANSF, alpha=0.12)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("density / weight")
ax.set_title(r"$M(t)$ is the total area under the tilted curve")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Here is the second mental image, the one behind "moments by differentiation." The
exponential has the Taylor series $e^{z} = 1 + z + \tfrac{z^2}{2!} + \tfrac{z^3}{3!} +
\cdots$. Put $z = tX$:

$$
e^{tX} = 1 + tX + \frac{t^2}{2!}X^2 + \frac{t^3}{3!}X^3 + \cdots ,
$$

a power series in $t$ whose coefficients are exactly the powers of $X$. Averaging term by
term, $M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]$ becomes a power series in $t$ whose coefficients are the
**moments** $E[X^k]$. A power series is a barcode: each moment sits on its own power of
$t$. Differentiating at $t=0$ is just the standard way of reading a single coefficient off
a power series. That is the whole mechanism, and we make it rigorous next.

# The moment generating function {#sec-mgf}

## Definition {#sec-mgf-def}

::: {#def-mgf}
**Definition (moment generating function).** For a random variable $X$, the **moment
generating function** is the function of a real variable $t$ defined by

$$
M_X(t) \;=\; E\!\left[e^{tX}\right]
\;=\;
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle \sum_{x} e^{tx}\, p_X(x), & X \text{ discrete with pmf } p_X,\\[2ex]
\displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{tx}\, f_X(x)\, dx, & X \text{ continuous with pdf } f_X,
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-mgf-def}

for those $t$ at which the sum or integral is finite. We say the MGF **exists** if it is
finite for all $t$ in some open interval $(-h, h)$ containing $0$ (with $h>0$).
:::

Three symbols to fix. $t$ is an ordinary real number — the **transform variable**, a
dial we turn; it is not random and has no probabilistic meaning of its own. $e^{tX}$ is
the random variable obtained by applying the deterministic function $u \mapsto e^{tu}$ to
$X$; computing its expectation is exactly the **law of the unconscious statistician** from
[PROB-05], which says $E[g(X)] = \sum_x g(x) p_X(x)$ or $\int g(x) f_X(x)\,dx$ — here with
$g(u)=e^{tu}$. That is why @eq-mgf-def has the form it does: it is LOTUS applied to the
exponential. Finally, note $M_X(0) = E[e^{0}] = E[1] = 1$ always — every MGF passes
through the point $(0,1)$, a free sanity check on any computation.

The clause about an *open interval around $0$* is not pedantry. The entire payoff —
generating moments by differentiation — needs the function to be defined and
differentiable in a neighborhood of $0$. Distributions whose MGF is finite only at the
single point $t=0$ (where it is trivially $1$) generate no moments and are treated as
**not having an MGF**. That distinction drives the whole back half of the article.

## Moments by differentiation {#sec-moments-deriv}

We now prove the headline property: the derivatives of $M_X$ at $0$ are the moments of
$X$. We give the clean power-series argument, the way it actually lands in practice.

::: {#thm-moments}
**Theorem (the MGF generates moments).** Suppose $M_X(t)$ is finite on an open interval
$(-h,h)$ around $0$. Then all moments $E[X^k]$ exist (are finite), $M_X$ has derivatives
of every order at $0$, and

$$
M_X^{(k)}(0) \;=\; E\!\left[X^k\right], \qquad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$ {#eq-moments}

Equivalently, $M_X$ has the Taylor expansion about $0$

$$
M_X(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{E[X^k]}{k!}\, t^k
\;=\; 1 + E[X]\,t + \frac{E[X^2]}{2!}\,t^2 + \frac{E[X^3]}{3!}\,t^3 + \cdots .
$$ {#eq-mgf-series}
:::

::: {.proof}
Start from the Taylor series of the exponential, valid for every real number $z$:

$$
e^{z} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{k!}
= 1 + z + \frac{z^2}{2!} + \frac{z^3}{3!} + \cdots .
$$

Substitute $z = tX$. Since $(tX)^k = t^k X^k$ and $t^k$ is a constant (not random), we may
pull each $t^k/k!$ outside as a coefficient:

$$
e^{tX} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(tX)^k}{k!}
= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{t^k}{k!}\, X^k
= 1 + tX + \frac{t^2}{2!}X^2 + \frac{t^3}{3!}X^3 + \cdots .
$$

Now take expectations of both sides. Expectation is **linear** — the expected value of a
sum is the sum of the expected values, and constants come out front (this is the linearity
proved in [PROB-05], and for the infinite sum it is licensed by the finiteness of $M_X$ on
$(-h,h)$, which is exactly the analyst's condition for swapping $E$ and $\sum$; we flag
this interchange as the one step resting on a convergence theorem above the Calc-1 floor).
Applying $E[\cdot]$ term by term, and pulling out the constants $t^k/k!$:

$$
M_X(t) = E\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{t^k}{k!} X^k\right]
= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{t^k}{k!}\, E[X^k] .
$$

This is precisely @eq-mgf-series: a power series in $t$ whose coefficient on $t^k$ is
$E[X^k]/k!$. The fact that the series converges on $(-h,h)$ tells us each $E[X^k]$ is
finite — an infinite coefficient would make the series diverge.

Finally, recall the bridge between a power series and derivatives. If a function has the
convergent expansion $g(t) = \sum_{k=0}^\infty c_k t^k$ near $0$, then differentiating $k$
times and evaluating at $0$ isolates the $k$-th coefficient as $g^{(k)}(0) = k!\,c_k$ —
because each differentiation drops the power by one and brings down a factor, and at $t=0$
every term except the constant one vanishes. (Concretely: $g'(t) = c_1 + 2c_2 t + 3 c_3
t^2 + \cdots$, so $g'(0)=c_1$; $g''(t) = 2c_2 + 6 c_3 t + \cdots$, so $g''(0)=2c_2$; in
general $g^{(k)}(0) = k!\,c_k$.) Here $c_k = E[X^k]/k!$, so

$$
M_X^{(k)}(0) = k!\, c_k = k!\cdot \frac{E[X^k]}{k!} = E[X^k],
$$

which is @eq-moments. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So the recipe is mechanical: write down $M_X(t)$, differentiate, set $t=0$. The first
derivative gives the mean, the second gives $E[X^2]$ (and then $\operatorname{Var}(X) =
E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ by the computational identity of [PROB-06]), the third gives $E[X^3]$,
and so on — a sequence of integrals replaced by a sequence of derivatives.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why differentiate at $t=0$ specifically, and not somewhere else?"* Because the moments
are the Taylor coefficients of $M_X$ **about $0$**, and the Taylor coefficients about a
point are read off by differentiating *at that point*. Evaluating $M_X'$ at any other $t$
gives $E[X e^{tX}]$ — a *tilted* moment, weighted toward one tail — not the plain moment
$E[X]$. Only at $t=0$ does the tilt $e^{tX}$ become $e^0=1$ and drop away, leaving the
clean moment. The point $t=0$ is where the transform touches the original distribution
without distortion.
:::

# The MGFs of the catalog {#sec-catalog}

Theory is cheap; let us compute. We work out the MGF of four workhorse distributions from
the catalog, deriving each moment-by-differentiation result so nothing is asserted. These
also become the raw material for the convolution results in @sec-convolution.

## Bernoulli and binomial {#sec-mgf-bernoulli}

Let $X \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$: it takes the value $1$ with probability $p$ and $0$ with
probability $1-p$ (the single yes/no trial of [PROB-11]). By @eq-mgf-def, summing over the
two possible values $x\in\{0,1\}$,

$$
M_X(t) = \sum_{x\in\{0,1\}} e^{tx} p_X(x)
= e^{t\cdot 0}(1-p) + e^{t\cdot 1} p
= (1-p) + p\,e^{t},
$$ {#eq-mgf-bernoulli}

which is finite for every real $t$ (it is a finite sum), so the MGF exists on all of
$\mathbb R$. Check the moments. Differentiating, $M_X'(t) = p\,e^t$, so $M_X'(0) = p\cdot
1 = p = E[X]$ — correct, a Bernoulli's mean is $p$. Again, $M_X''(t) = p\,e^t$, so
$M_X''(0) = p = E[X^2]$. Then $\operatorname{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = p - p^2 =
p(1-p)$, matching [PROB-11] exactly. Every moment of a Bernoulli equals $p$ because
$X^k = X$ for $X\in\{0,1\}$, and indeed every derivative of @eq-mgf-bernoulli is $p\,e^t$.

Now $Y \sim \text{Binomial}(n,p)$, the number of successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli
trials. Rather than sum $e^{ty}\binom{n}{y}p^y(1-p)^{n-y}$ directly (doable, but it asks
for the binomial theorem), we will get its MGF in one line from the convolution property
in @sec-binomial-mgf — the cleaner route. For now, record the Bernoulli result.

## Poisson {#sec-mgf-poisson}

Let $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, the count of rare events with pmf $p_X(k) =
e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!$ for $k=0,1,2,\dots$ (from [PROB-12]). Then

$$
M_X(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} e^{tk}\, \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}
= e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(e^{t}\lambda)^k}{k!} .
$$

The sum is the Taylor series of the exponential evaluated at $e^t\lambda$: recall
$\sum_{k=0}^\infty a^k/k! = e^{a}$, here with $a = e^t\lambda$. So the sum equals
$e^{\lambda e^{t}}$, and

$$
M_X(t) = e^{-\lambda}\, e^{\lambda e^{t}} = \exp\!\bigl(\lambda(e^{t} - 1)\bigr),
$$ {#eq-mgf-poisson}

finite for all real $t$ (the series converges for every $a$). Check the mean. Using the
chain rule, $\frac{d}{dt}\exp(\lambda(e^t-1)) = \exp(\lambda(e^t-1))\cdot \lambda e^t$, so

$$
M_X'(t) = \lambda e^{t}\exp\!\bigl(\lambda(e^t-1)\bigr),
\qquad
M_X'(0) = \lambda\cdot 1\cdot \exp(0) = \lambda = E[X].
$$

A Poisson's mean is $\lambda$ — correct. For the variance, differentiate again with the
product rule on $\lambda e^t \cdot M_X(t)$:

$$
M_X''(t) = \lambda e^t M_X(t) + \lambda e^t\, M_X'(t)
= \lambda e^t M_X(t)\bigl(1 + \lambda e^t\bigr).
$$

At $t=0$: $M_X''(0) = \lambda\cdot 1 \cdot 1\cdot(1+\lambda) = \lambda + \lambda^2 =
E[X^2]$. Then $\operatorname{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = (\lambda+\lambda^2) - \lambda^2
= \lambda$ — the famous equidispersion (mean equals variance) of [PROB-12], dropping out
of the transform in two derivatives.

## Normal {#sec-mgf-normal}

Let $X \sim \mathcal N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, with density $f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\!\bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\bigr)$ from [PROB-13]. We compute

$$
M_X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{tx}\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr)\, dx .
$$

Combine the two exponentials and work the exponent. Write the exponent as

$$
tx - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\Bigl[(x-\mu)^2 - 2\sigma^2 t x\Bigr].
$$

We **complete the square** in $x$ inside the brackets. Expand $(x-\mu)^2 = x^2 - 2\mu x +
\mu^2$, so the bracket is $x^2 - 2\mu x + \mu^2 - 2\sigma^2 t x = x^2 - 2(\mu + \sigma^2
t)x + \mu^2$. To complete the square we add and subtract $(\mu+\sigma^2 t)^2$:

$$
x^2 - 2(\mu+\sigma^2 t)x + \mu^2
= \bigl(x - (\mu+\sigma^2 t)\bigr)^2 - (\mu+\sigma^2 t)^2 + \mu^2 .
$$

Expand the two correction terms: $-(\mu+\sigma^2 t)^2 + \mu^2 = -\mu^2 - 2\mu\sigma^2 t -
\sigma^4 t^2 + \mu^2 = -2\mu\sigma^2 t - \sigma^4 t^2$. Therefore the full exponent is

$$
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\Bigl[\bigl(x-(\mu+\sigma^2 t)\bigr)^2 - 2\mu\sigma^2 t - \sigma^4 t^2\Bigr]
= -\frac{\bigl(x-(\mu+\sigma^2 t)\bigr)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2},
$$

where the last two terms came from $-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\cdot(-2\mu\sigma^2 t) = \mu t$
and $-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\cdot(-\sigma^4 t^2) = \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}$. Pull the two
$x$-free terms out of the integral as constants:

$$
M_X(t) = \exp\!\Bigl(\mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\Bigr)
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{\bigl(x-(\mu+\sigma^2 t)\bigr)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr)\, dx .
$$

The remaining integrand is the density of a normal distribution with mean
$\mu + \sigma^2 t$ and the same variance $\sigma^2$. A probability density integrates to
$1$ over the whole line (the normalization established in [PROB-13] via the Gaussian
integral), so the integral equals $1$ regardless of where its center sits. We are left
with

$$
\boxed{\;M_X(t) = \exp\!\Bigl(\mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\Bigr),\;}
$$ {#eq-mgf-normal}

finite for every real $t$. The MGF of a normal is itself the exponential of a quadratic in
$t$ — a fact we will exploit relentlessly in @sec-sum-normals. Verify the moments.
Differentiating with the chain rule, $M_X'(t) = (\mu + \sigma^2 t)\exp(\mu t +
\tfrac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2})$, so $M_X'(0) = \mu\cdot 1 = \mu = E[X]$. The product rule gives
$M_X''(t) = \sigma^2 M_X(t) + (\mu+\sigma^2 t)^2 M_X(t)$, hence $M_X''(0) = \sigma^2 +
\mu^2 = E[X^2]$, so $\operatorname{Var}(X) = (\sigma^2 + \mu^2) - \mu^2 = \sigma^2$.
Mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$ — exactly as named.

A special case worth recording: the **standard** normal $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ has $\mu=0,
\sigma^2=1$, so $M_Z(t) = e^{t^2/2}$.

## Exponential (and a preview of gamma) {#sec-mgf-exponential}

Let $X \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda)$, the memoryless waiting time with density
$f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x\ge 0$ (rate parameterization, from [PROB-14]).
Then

$$
M_X(t) = \int_0^{\infty} e^{tx}\, \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx
= \lambda \int_0^{\infty} e^{-(\lambda - t)x}\, dx .
$$

The integral $\int_0^\infty e^{-cx}\,dx$ converges only when $c>0$, and then equals
$1/c$. (Compute it: $\int_0^\infty e^{-cx}\,dx = [-\tfrac{1}{c}e^{-cx}]_0^\infty = 0 -
(-\tfrac1c) = \tfrac1c$, valid because $e^{-cx}\to 0$ as $x\to\infty$ exactly when $c>0$.)
Here $c = \lambda - t$, so we need $\lambda - t > 0$, i.e. $t < \lambda$:

$$
\boxed{\;M_X(t) = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}, \qquad t < \lambda.\;}
$$ {#eq-mgf-exponential}

This is the first MGF that does **not** exist for all $t$ — it is finite only on the
interval $(-\infty, \lambda)$ and blows up as $t\uparrow\lambda$. But that interval
contains an open neighborhood of $0$ (since $\lambda>0$), so the MGF still exists in the
sense of @def-mgf and still generates moments. Check: $M_X'(t) =
\frac{d}{dt}\lambda(\lambda-t)^{-1} = \lambda(\lambda-t)^{-2}$, so $M_X'(0) =
\lambda/\lambda^2 = 1/\lambda = E[X]$ — the exponential's mean is $1/\lambda$, correct.
And $M_X''(t) = 2\lambda(\lambda-t)^{-3}$, so $M_X''(0) = 2\lambda/\lambda^3 = 2/\lambda^2
= E[X^2]$, giving $\operatorname{Var}(X) = 2/\lambda^2 - 1/\lambda^2 = 1/\lambda^2$ — also
correct.

The gamma generalizes this. A $\text{Gamma}(\alpha,\lambda)$ variable (shape $\alpha$,
rate $\lambda$) is a sum of $\alpha$ independent exponentials when $\alpha$ is an integer;
we will recover its MGF $\bigl(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda-t}\bigr)^\alpha$ for free from the
convolution property in @sec-gamma-mgf, rather than grinding the gamma-function integral.

Let us see these four transforms side by side, and confirm the existence boundary of the
exponential's, with a figure.

```{python}
#| label: fig-catalog-mgfs
#| fig-cap: 'The MGFs of four catalog distributions, each plotted over the range of $t$ where it is finite. Bernoulli, Poisson, and normal MGFs are finite for every $t$. The exponential''s MGF $\lambda/(\lambda-t)$ (here $\lambda=1.5$) is finite only for $t<\lambda$ and diverges to $+\infty$ as $t\to\lambda$ (dashed vertical line) — the first sign that existence is not automatic. Every curve passes through $(0,1)$, the universal value $M_X(0)=1$ (grey dot).'
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8.6, 6.0))

# Bernoulli(0.4)
t = np.linspace(-3, 3, 200); p = 0.4
axes[0,0].plot(t, (1-p) + p*np.exp(t), color=TRANSF)
axes[0,0].set_title(r"Bernoulli$(0.4)$:  $(1-p)+pe^{t}$", fontsize=10)

# Poisson(2)
t = np.linspace(-3, 1.2, 200); lam = 2.0
axes[0,1].plot(t, np.exp(lam*(np.exp(t)-1)), color=TRANSF)
axes[0,1].set_title(r"Poisson$(2)$:  $e^{\lambda(e^{t}-1)}$", fontsize=10)

# Normal(0,1)
t = np.linspace(-3, 3, 200)
axes[1,0].plot(t, np.exp(t**2/2), color=TRANSF)
axes[1,0].set_title(r"Normal$(0,1)$:  $e^{t^{2}/2}$", fontsize=10)

# Exponential(1.5): finite only for t<1.5
lam = 1.5
t = np.linspace(-3, 1.45, 200)
axes[1,1].plot(t, lam/(lam - t), color=TRANSF)
axes[1,1].axvline(lam, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4)
axes[1,1].text(lam-0.05, 8, r"$t=\lambda$", ha="right", color=GREY, fontsize=9)
axes[1,1].set_ylim(0, 11)
axes[1,1].set_title(r"Exponential$(1.5)$:  $\lambda/(\lambda-t)$", fontsize=10)

for ax in axes.ravel():
    ax.scatter([0],[1], color=GREY, zorder=5, s=30)
    ax.set_xlabel("$t$"); ax.set_ylabel("$M(t)$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Uniqueness: the MGF is a fingerprint {#sec-uniqueness}

Generating moments is only half the value. The other half is that the MGF, *when it exists
in a neighborhood of zero*, determines the distribution completely — so if we recognize the
MGF of a sum as the MGF of a known distribution, we have identified the distribution of
that sum. This is the property that makes the convolution trick of @sec-convolution
actually conclude something.

::: {#thm-uniqueness}
**Theorem (uniqueness).** Let $X$ and $Y$ be random variables whose MGFs both exist and
satisfy $M_X(t) = M_Y(t)$ for all $t$ in some open interval $(-h, h)$ around $0$. Then $X$
and $Y$ have the **same distribution** (the same CDF).
:::

We do not prove this theorem in full — a complete proof requires the inversion of the
Laplace/Fourier transform, machinery that lives above our floor and is owned by the
analysis underlying [PROB-21]. But we can explain *why it is true* and exactly what it
rests on, so it is not a black box. The MGF on an interval determines, by @thm-moments,
every moment $E[X^k]$. For distributions whose moments do not grow too fast — which is
guaranteed precisely by the MGF being finite on an open interval — the moment sequence
pins down the distribution uniquely (this is the resolution of the classical *moment
problem*). Equivalently, the MGF is a Laplace transform of the distribution, and the
Laplace transform is invertible: knowing $M_X$ on a neighborhood of $0$ lets one recover
$f_X$ by an inversion formula. Either way, *equal MGFs near zero force equal
distributions.* We flag the inversion theorem as the imported ingredient and use the
conclusion.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Uniqueness needs the MGF to exist on an open interval around $0$ — not at a single
point, and not the moments alone.** Two distinct distributions can share *every* finite
moment yet differ, when neither has an MGF on an interval (the standard example is the
log-normal versus certain tilted log-normals — same moments, different densities). The
moment sequence alone is *not* always a fingerprint; the MGF being finite near $0$ is the
extra condition that makes it one. This is the deep reason we kept insisting on "finite on
$(-h,h)$" in @def-mgf. When you invoke uniqueness in an interview, state the open-interval
hypothesis — leaving it out is the classic error.
:::

# Sums of independents: convolution becomes a product {#sec-convolution}

Here is the property that earns the MGF its keep. It rests on one fact about independence,
proved in [PROB-08] and restated here so the chain is complete.

## The independence-to-product lemma {#sec-indep-lemma}

::: {#lem-product}
**Lemma (expectation of a product factors under independence).** If $U$ and $V$ are
independent random variables, then for functions $g, h$ for which the expectations exist,

$$
E\bigl[g(U)\, h(V)\bigr] = E[g(U)]\, E[h(V)] .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
This is the defining consequence of independence established in [PROB-08]; we re-derive it
for the continuous case so nothing is assumed. Independence means the joint density
factors as $f_{U,V}(u,v) = f_U(u) f_V(v)$. Then, by LOTUS for two variables,

$$
E[g(U) h(V)]
= \iint g(u) h(v)\, f_{U,V}(u,v)\, du\, dv
= \iint g(u) h(v)\, f_U(u) f_V(v)\, du\, dv .
$$

The double integral separates because the integrand is a product of a $u$-only factor and
a $v$-only factor, and the region is the whole plane (a product region):

$$
= \Bigl(\int g(u) f_U(u)\, du\Bigr)\Bigl(\int h(v) f_V(v)\, dv\Bigr)
= E[g(U)]\, E[h(V)] .
$$

The discrete case is identical with sums in place of integrals. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

## The convolution theorem for MGFs {#sec-conv-thm}

::: {#thm-conv}
**Theorem (MGF of a sum of independents).** If $X_1, \dots, X_n$ are **independent** random
variables, each with an MGF on a common interval around $0$, and $S = X_1 + \cdots + X_n$,
then

$$
M_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_{X_i}(t)
= M_{X_1}(t)\, M_{X_2}(t)\cdots M_{X_n}(t) .
$$ {#eq-conv}

In particular, if the $X_i$ are **i.i.d.** with common MGF $M_X$, then $M_S(t) =
\bigl[M_X(t)\bigr]^n$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Take $n=2$ first; the general case is induction. By the definition of the MGF and the key
algebraic fact $e^{t(X_1+X_2)} = e^{tX_1}\, e^{tX_2}$ (the exponential turns a sum in the
exponent into a product — this is *the* property we built the whole transform around):

$$
M_S(t) = E\bigl[e^{t(X_1 + X_2)}\bigr] = E\bigl[e^{tX_1}\, e^{tX_2}\bigr].
$$

Now $e^{tX_1}$ is a function of $X_1$ alone and $e^{tX_2}$ a function of $X_2$ alone, and
$X_1, X_2$ are independent, so @lem-product (with $g(u)=h(u)=e^{tu}$) applies:

$$
E\bigl[e^{tX_1}\, e^{tX_2}\bigr] = E\bigl[e^{tX_1}\bigr]\, E\bigl[e^{tX_2}\bigr]
= M_{X_1}(t)\, M_{X_2}(t).
$$

For general $n$, write $S = (X_1 + \cdots + X_{n-1}) + X_n$. The sum $X_1+\cdots+X_{n-1}$
is independent of $X_n$ (a function of the first $n-1$ independent variables is independent
of the $n$-th — the grouping property of independence from [PROB-08]), so the two-variable
result applies and, by the induction hypothesis, $M_{X_1+\cdots+X_{n-1}}(t) =
\prod_{i=1}^{n-1} M_{X_i}(t)$. Multiplying by $M_{X_n}(t)$ gives @eq-conv. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That is the whole mechanism: the messy convolution integral @eq-convolution for the
density of a sum is replaced by *multiplying* MGFs. Then — and this is where uniqueness
earns its keep — if the product happens to be the MGF of a recognizable distribution,
@thm-uniqueness lets us conclude that the sum *has* that distribution. We now cash this in
four times.

## Sum of independent normals is normal {#sec-sum-normals}

Let $X_1 \sim \mathcal N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $X_2 \sim \mathcal N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$
be independent, and $S = X_1 + X_2$. Multiply their MGFs from @eq-mgf-normal:

$$
M_S(t) = M_{X_1}(t)\, M_{X_2}(t)
= \exp\!\Bigl(\mu_1 t + \tfrac{\sigma_1^2 t^2}{2}\Bigr)
  \exp\!\Bigl(\mu_2 t + \tfrac{\sigma_2^2 t^2}{2}\Bigr).
$$

Add the exponents (a product of exponentials adds what is upstairs):

$$
M_S(t) = \exp\!\Bigl((\mu_1 + \mu_2)\, t + \tfrac{(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)\, t^2}{2}\Bigr).
$$

Compare with @eq-mgf-normal: this is *exactly* the MGF of a normal with mean
$\mu_1+\mu_2$ and variance $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2$. By uniqueness (@thm-uniqueness),

$$
X_1 + X_2 \sim \mathcal N\bigl(\mu_1 + \mu_2,\; \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2\bigr).
$$

The closure-under-addition of the normal — which [PROB-13] could only assert or prove with
a hard convolution — falls out in three lines of algebra. By induction, a sum of any number
of independent normals is normal, with means and variances adding. (Note the means always
add by linearity of expectation, and the variances add by independence — exactly the rules
of [PROB-08]; the MGF additionally certifies that the *shape* stays normal.)

## Sum of independent Poissons is Poisson {#sec-sum-poisson}

Let $X_1\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda_2)$ be
independent. Multiply @eq-mgf-poisson:

$$
M_S(t) = \exp\!\bigl(\lambda_1(e^t-1)\bigr)\exp\!\bigl(\lambda_2(e^t-1)\bigr)
= \exp\!\bigl((\lambda_1+\lambda_2)(e^t - 1)\bigr),
$$

which is the MGF of a $\text{Poisson}(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)$. By uniqueness, $X_1 + X_2 \sim
\text{Poisson}(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)$: independent Poisson counts add into a Poisson whose
rate is the sum of the rates. This is the additivity flagged in [PROB-12], now proved in one
line.

## Sum of Bernoullis is binomial {#sec-binomial-mgf}

Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d. $\text{Bernoulli}(p)$, and $Y = \sum_i X_i$ their sum — which
*is*, by definition, $\text{Binomial}(n,p)$. By @thm-conv with the common MGF
@eq-mgf-bernoulli,

$$
M_Y(t) = \bigl[M_{X_1}(t)\bigr]^n = \bigl[(1-p) + p\,e^t\bigr]^n .
$$ {#eq-mgf-binomial}

So this is the MGF of the binomial, obtained without ever touching the binomial-theorem
sum we sidestepped in @sec-mgf-bernoulli. Read its moments to confirm the binomial's mean
and variance. Differentiating @eq-mgf-binomial with the chain rule (the outer power
$(\cdot)^n$ brings down $n(\cdot)^{n-1}$, times the derivative $p e^t$ of the inside),

$$
M_Y'(t) = n\bigl[(1-p)+pe^t\bigr]^{n-1}\cdot p e^t = np\,e^t\bigl[(1-p)+pe^t\bigr]^{n-1}.
$$

At $t=0$ the bracket is $(1-p)+p=1$, so $M_Y'(0) = np\cdot 1\cdot 1^{n-1} = np = E[Y]$. For
the second derivative, write $M_Y'(t) = np\cdot u(t)\, v(t)$ with $u(t)=e^t$ and
$v(t)=\bigl[(1-p)+pe^t\bigr]^{n-1}$, and apply the **product rule** $(uv)' = u'v + uv'$.
Here $u'(t)=e^t$, and $v'(t) = (n-1)\bigl[(1-p)+pe^t\bigr]^{n-2}\cdot p e^t$ by the chain
rule again. Thus

$$
M_Y''(t) = np\Bigl[\,e^t\bigl[(1-p)+pe^t\bigr]^{n-1}
+ e^t\,(n-1)\bigl[(1-p)+pe^t\bigr]^{n-2} p e^t\,\Bigr].
$$

Evaluate at $t=0$, where $e^0=1$ and the bracket $(1-p)+p=1$, so every power of it is $1$:

$$
M_Y''(0) = np\bigl[\,1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot(n-1)\cdot 1\cdot p\cdot 1\,\bigr]
= np\bigl[1 + (n-1)p\bigr] = np + n(n-1)p^2 = E[Y^2].
$$

Then, by the computational identity of [PROB-06],

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y) = M_Y''(0) - \bigl(M_Y'(0)\bigr)^2
= np + n(n-1)p^2 - (np)^2
= np + n^2 p^2 - n p^2 - n^2 p^2
= np - np^2 = np(1-p),
$$

the binomial mean $np$ and variance $np(1-p)$ of [PROB-11], confirmed.

## Sum of exponentials is gamma {#sec-gamma-mgf}

Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d. $\text{Exponential}(\lambda)$, and $S = \sum_i X_i$. By
@thm-conv with the common MGF @eq-mgf-exponential,

$$
M_S(t) = \Bigl[\frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}\Bigr]^n
= \Bigl(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}\Bigr)^n, \qquad t < \lambda.
$$ {#eq-mgf-gamma}

This is the MGF of a $\text{Gamma}(n, \lambda)$ distribution (shape $n$, rate $\lambda$).
By uniqueness, the sum of $n$ i.i.d. exponentials with rate $\lambda$ is exactly
$\text{Gamma}(n,\lambda)$ — the "sum of waiting times" fact of [PROB-14], now certified by
the transform without convolving $n-1$ times. (And $M_S'(0)$ gives the gamma mean
$n/\lambda$; we leave that one-step differentiation as a check the reader can now do
reflexively.)

@fig-convolution shows the convolution-becomes-product story directly: the histogram of a
simulated sum lands precisely on the density predicted by multiplying MGFs.

```{python}
#| label: fig-convolution
#| fig-cap: 'Convolution-by-multiplication, verified by simulation. We add $n=5$ i.i.d. Exponential($\lambda=1$) variables. Multiplying their MGFs predicts the sum is Gamma$(5,1)$, by @eq-mgf-gamma and uniqueness. The blue histogram is $200{,}000$ simulated sums; the red curve is the Gamma$(5,1)$ density the MGF predicted. They coincide — the product of MGFs found the distribution of the sum without ever computing a convolution integral.'
from scipy import stats
rng = np.random.default_rng(20)
n_terms, n_sims, lam = 5, 200_000, 1.0
sums = rng.exponential(scale=1/lam, size=(n_sims, n_terms)).sum(axis=1)

xs = np.linspace(0, 16, 300)
gamma_pdf = stats.gamma.pdf(xs, a=n_terms, scale=1/lam)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(sums, bins=80, density=True, color=DENS, alpha=0.45,
        label=f"histogram of {n_sims:,} simulated sums")
ax.plot(xs, gamma_pdf, color=TRANSF,
        label=r"Gamma$(5,1)$ density (predicted by $\prod M_{X_i}$)")
ax.set_xlabel("$s = X_1 + \\cdots + X_5$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Sum of 5 i.i.d. Exponentials is Gamma$(5,1)$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You showed sums of independent normals/Poissons/gammas stay in the same family. Does
every distribution do that?"* No — this **closure under addition** is special. Bernoulli
is the cleanest counterexample: the sum of two independent Bernoullis is *not* a Bernoulli
(it can equal $2$), it is a binomial — the family changed. Uniform plus uniform is
triangular, not uniform. The MGF tells you *which* families are closed: a family is closed
under independent addition exactly when multiplying two of its MGFs lands you back at an
MGF of the same form, as it did for normal ($e^{\text{quadratic}}$ times
$e^{\text{quadratic}}$ is $e^{\text{quadratic}}$) and Poisson. The normal, Poisson, gamma
(fixed rate), negative-binomial (fixed $p$), and Cauchy are the famous closed
("infinitely divisible"-adjacent) families; most are not closed.
:::

# When the MGF fails to exist {#sec-existence}

We have been careful to say "when the MGF exists." Now we confront the cases where it does
not — because they are common, important, and the reason the characteristic function had
to be invented.

## The mechanism of failure: heavy tails {#sec-heavy-tails}

The integral $\int e^{tx} f(x)\,dx$ asks $e^{tx}$ — which grows *exponentially* in $x$ —
to be tamed by the tail of the density $f(x)$. If the density decays only
*polynomially* (like $1/x^{p}$) rather than at least exponentially, then for any $t>0$ the
product $e^{tx}\cdot x^{-p}$ still blows up as $x\to\infty$, the integral diverges, and
$M_X(t) = +\infty$. Such distributions are called **heavy-tailed**, and for them the MGF is
finite only at $t=0$ (where the integrand is just $f$, integrating to $1$) — which, by our
@def-mgf, means it does not exist.

The canonical example is the **Cauchy distribution**, with density $f(x) =
\frac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}$ on the whole real line. Its tails decay like $1/x^2$, so heavy that
even its *mean* does not exist (the integral $\int x f(x)\,dx$ diverges, as shown in
[PROB-05]). With no finite mean, there is certainly no MGF on an interval — there are no
moments for it to generate. @fig-heavy-tail contrasts a thin tail (normal) with a heavy
tail (Cauchy) and shows the tilted integrand diverging.

```{python}
#| label: fig-heavy-tail
#| fig-cap: 'Why heavy tails kill the MGF. **Left:** the normal tail (blue) decays like $e^{-x^2/2}$, fast enough that the tilted integrand $e^{tx}f(x)$ (red, $t=0.5$) still goes to zero — its area, the MGF, is finite. **Right:** the Cauchy tail decays only like $1/x^2$; the tilted integrand $e^{tx}f(x)$ (red) *grows without bound* as $x\to\infty$, so the area is infinite and the MGF does not exist. The exponential tilt always wins against a polynomial tail.'
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7))
t = 0.5

x = np.linspace(0, 8, 400)
fn = np.exp(-x**2/2)/np.sqrt(2*np.pi)
axL.plot(x, fn, color=DENS, label=r"normal tail $f(x)$")
axL.plot(x, np.exp(t*x)*fn, color=TRANSF, ls="--", label=r"$e^{tx}f(x)$")
axL.set_title("Thin tail: tilted integrand decays"); axL.set_xlabel("$x$")
axL.set_ylabel("density / weight"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
axL.set_ylim(0, 0.6)

x = np.linspace(0, 8, 400)
fc = 1/(np.pi*(1+x**2))
axR.plot(x, fc, color=DENS, label=r"Cauchy tail $f(x)$")
axR.plot(x, np.exp(t*x)*fc, color=TRANSF, ls="--", label=r"$e^{tx}f(x)$ (diverges)")
axR.set_title("Heavy tail: tilted integrand explodes"); axR.set_xlabel("$x$")
axR.set_ylabel("density / weight"); axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper left")
axR.set_ylim(0, 2.0)
plt.tight_layout()
```

This is not an exotic corner. Heavy tails are the rule, not the exception, in finance
(asset returns), insurance (large claims), network traffic, and any setting governed by
power laws. A tool that silently requires light tails is a tool that fails exactly where
the interesting risk lives. We need a transform that survives heavy tails — and there is
one, with no cost in existence at all.

# The characteristic function {#sec-cf}

## Complex exponentials from scratch {#sec-complex}

The fix replaces the real exponent $tX$ with the *imaginary* exponent $itX$, where $i$ is
the imaginary unit with $i^2 = -1$. Because our floor is Calc 1, we build the one fact we
need. **Euler's formula** states

$$
e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta ,
$$ {#eq-euler}

for any real number $\theta$. You can take this as the *definition* of raising $e$ to an
imaginary power, and it is the natural one: substitute $z = i\theta$ into the Taylor series
$e^z = \sum z^k/k!$, use the cycle $i^0=1, i^1=i, i^2=-1, i^3=-i, i^4=1,\dots$ to split the
series into its real and imaginary parts, and the real part is exactly the Taylor series of
$\cos\theta$ while the imaginary part is that of $\sin\theta$. The single fact that makes
$e^{itX}$ tame is its **modulus** (its distance from the origin in the complex plane):

$$
\bigl|e^{i\theta}\bigr| = \sqrt{\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta} = \sqrt{1} = 1 ,
$$ {#eq-unit-modulus}

using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$. So $e^{i\theta}$ always lies *on the
unit circle*, no matter how large $\theta$ is. Where $e^{tX}$ could grow without bound and
break the integral, $e^{itX}$ never exceeds magnitude $1$ — it just rotates. That single
geometric fact is the entire reason the next definition always works.

## Definition and unconditional existence {#sec-cf-def}

::: {#def-cf}
**Definition (characteristic function).** For a random variable $X$, the **characteristic
function** is the complex-valued function of a real variable $t$,

$$
\varphi_X(t) \;=\; E\!\left[e^{itX}\right]
\;=\; E[\cos(tX)] + i\, E[\sin(tX)] ,
$$ {#eq-cf-def}

where the second form uses Euler's formula @eq-euler to split it into a real part
$E[\cos tX]$ and an imaginary part $E[\sin tX]$.
:::

::: {#thm-cf-exists}
**Theorem (the characteristic function always exists).** For *every* random variable $X$
and every real $t$, $\varphi_X(t)$ is finite, with $|\varphi_X(t)| \le 1$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The expectation of a complex quantity is bounded in modulus by the expectation of its
modulus — the triangle inequality, carried through the integral/sum:
$\bigl|E[e^{itX}]\bigr| \le E\bigl[\,|e^{itX}|\,\bigr]$. By @eq-unit-modulus, $|e^{itX}| =
1$ for every value of $X$ and every $t$. Therefore

$$
\bigl|\varphi_X(t)\bigr| \le E\bigl[\,|e^{itX}|\,\bigr] = E[1] = 1 .
$$

A quantity bounded by $1$ is certainly finite. No assumption on the tails of $X$ was used —
not even that $X$ has a mean. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the payoff. The characteristic function exists for the Cauchy, for any
power-law-tailed distribution, for anything at all — because the cosine and sine are bounded
between $-1$ and $1$, so their expectations always exist. We pay exactly one price: the
output is now a complex number. Everything else carries over.

## All the good properties survive {#sec-cf-props}

The characteristic function inherits the MGF's three pillars, with the real exponential
swapped for the complex one.

**Moments by differentiation.** When $X$ has $k$ finite moments, differentiating
@eq-cf-def $k$ times at $0$ gives

$$
\varphi_X^{(k)}(0) = i^k\, E[X^k],
\qquad\text{e.g.}\quad
\varphi_X'(0) = i\,E[X], \quad \varphi_X''(0) = i^2 E[X^2] = -E[X^2].
$$ {#eq-cf-moments}

The derivation is identical to @thm-moments with $z=itX$: the Taylor series gives
$\varphi_X(t) = \sum_k \frac{(it)^k}{k!}E[X^k]$, and reading off the $k$-th coefficient
brings down $i^k E[X^k]$. The only change from @eq-moments is the bookkeeping factor $i^k$,
which you divide back out. (For the Cauchy, $\varphi$ exists but is not differentiable at
$0$ — consistent with its having no mean.)

**Convolution becomes a product.** The proof of @thm-conv used only the functional equation
$e^{a+b}=e^a e^b$ and the factoring of @lem-product, both of which hold verbatim for
$e^{it(\cdot)}$. So for independent $X_1,\dots,X_n$ with $S=\sum_i X_i$,

$$
\varphi_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^n \varphi_{X_i}(t) .
$$ {#eq-cf-conv}

**Uniqueness.** This is now an *unconditional theorem* — no interval hypothesis is needed,
because $\varphi_X$ is always defined on all of $\mathbb R$.

::: {#thm-cf-uniqueness}
**Theorem (uniqueness for characteristic functions).** Two random variables have the same
distribution **if and only if** they have the same characteristic function for all real
$t$.
:::

This is the cleaner sibling of @thm-uniqueness, and it holds with no fine print: the
characteristic function is the Fourier transform of the distribution, and the Fourier
transform is always invertible (Lévy's inversion formula recovers the CDF from $\varphi$).
We flag the inversion theorem as the imported analysis result and use the conclusion. The
absence of the open-interval caveat is exactly why the characteristic function, not the MGF,
is the rigorous tool in advanced probability.

## A worked characteristic function: the standard normal {#sec-cf-normal}

For the standard normal $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$, the MGF was $M_Z(t)=e^{t^2/2}$. The
characteristic function is obtained by the same complete-the-square computation as
@sec-mgf-normal with $t$ replaced by $it$, and the result is

$$
\varphi_Z(t) = e^{(it)^2/2} = e^{-t^2/2} .
$$ {#eq-cf-normal}

The substitution $t\to it$ is rigorous here because the normal's MGF is *entire* (finite
for all complex arguments), so the formula extends from the real axis to the imaginary one
without trouble — a convenience the normal enjoys precisely because it has no heavy tail.
Note the sign flip: $(it)^2 = i^2 t^2 = -t^2$, so the characteristic function is
$e^{-t^2/2}$, a real, bounded, bell-shaped function of $t$ (the Gaussian is, beautifully,
its own Fourier transform up to scaling). General $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ likewise has
$\varphi(t) = \exp(i\mu t - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2 t^2)$.

And the Cauchy, which had *no* MGF at all, has the perfectly well-defined characteristic
function $\varphi(t) = e^{-|t|}$. @fig-cf shows both, real and bounded, existing where the
MGF could not.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cf
#| fig-cap: 'Characteristic functions exist where MGFs do not. **Left:** the standard normal''s characteristic function $\varphi(t)=e^{-t^2/2}$ (red), bounded by $1$ and tending to $0$ — compare its MGF $e^{t^2/2}$ (grey), which blows up. **Right:** the Cauchy has *no* MGF (it would be infinite for every $t\neq 0$), yet its characteristic function $\varphi(t)=e^{-|t|}$ (red) is a clean, bounded function. The complex exponential''s unit modulus guarantees the curve never leaves the band $[-1,1]$ (grey dashed).'
t = np.linspace(-4, 4, 400)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7))
axL.plot(t, np.exp(-t**2/2), color=TRANSF, label=r"$\varphi(t)=e^{-t^2/2}$ (always finite)")
axL.plot(t, np.minimum(np.exp(t**2/2), 8), color=GREY, ls="--",
         label=r"MGF $e^{t^2/2}$ (blows up)")
axL.axhline(1, color=GREY, lw=0.8, ls=":"); axL.axhline(-1, color=GREY, lw=0.8, ls=":")
axL.set_ylim(-1.4, 8); axL.set_title("Standard normal"); axL.set_xlabel("$t$")
axL.set_ylabel("value"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper center")

axR.plot(t, np.exp(-np.abs(t)), color=TRANSF, label=r"$\varphi(t)=e^{-|t|}$")
axR.axhline(1, color=GREY, lw=0.8, ls=":"); axR.axhline(-1, color=GREY, lw=0.8, ls=":")
axR.set_ylim(-1.4, 1.4); axR.set_title("Cauchy (no MGF exists)"); axR.set_xlabel("$t$")
axR.set_ylabel("value"); axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the characteristic function always exists and the MGF doesn't, why teach the MGF at
all?"* Three honest reasons. **(1) No complex numbers.** The MGF is real-valued, so its
algebra and its derivatives are the ordinary Calc-1 ones — for the light-tailed
distributions you meet most often (normal, gamma, Poisson, binomial), it is simply easier
to compute and read. **(2) Tail behavior.** The MGF's *existence* on an interval is itself
information: it certifies that all moments are finite and the tails are at most
exponential, and it powers the **Chernoff bound** $P(X\ge a)\le e^{-ta}M_X(t)$ — the
exponential concentration inequality (a relative of Chebyshev from [PROB-19]) that the
characteristic function cannot give. **(3) Pedagogy.** The MGF teaches the moment-generating
and convolution ideas without the overhead of the complex plane; the characteristic
function is then "the same idea, made bulletproof." The right one-liner: *use the MGF when
it exists; reach for the characteristic function when it doesn't, or when you need an
airtight uniqueness/convergence argument.*
:::

# The link to the central limit theorem {#sec-clt-link}

We can now see, without proving it, *why* these transforms are the engine of the central
limit theorem — the result the Statistical Inference track ([INFR-A2]) owns and proves, and
the reason this article is its prerequisite.

The CLT concerns the standardized sum of $n$ i.i.d. variables with mean $\mu$ and variance
$\sigma^2$,

$$
Z_n = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu),
$$

and claims its distribution approaches a standard normal as $n\to\infty$. The
characteristic function turns this into a limit we can *see*. Because the summands are
i.i.d., @eq-cf-conv says $\varphi_{Z_n}(t)$ is a *power* of one summand's characteristic
function. Expanding that single characteristic function to second order via @eq-cf-moments
— its value at $0$ is $1$, its first derivative vanishes after centering, and its second
derivative is $-\sigma^2$ — gives, for the standardized centered variable,
$\varphi(t/(\sigma\sqrt n)) \approx 1 - \frac{t^2}{2n} + (\text{smaller})$. Raising to the
$n$-th power and using the calculus limit $\bigl(1 - \frac{a}{n}\bigr)^n \to e^{-a}$,

$$
\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \Bigl[\varphi\bigl(\tfrac{t}{\sigma\sqrt n}\bigr)\Bigr]^n
\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\; e^{-t^2/2} ,
$$

which is exactly the standard normal's characteristic function @eq-cf-normal. By the
*continuity theorem* — convergence of characteristic functions is convergence in
distribution, the Lévy result mentioned in @sec-why-history — $Z_n$ converges in
distribution to $\mathcal N(0,1)$. That continuity theorem is defined and used carefully in
[PROB-21] and the full CLT is assembled in [INFR-A2]; here we only point out that **the
characteristic function is the object that makes the limit a one-line calculation** — the
$e^{-t^2/2}$ at the end is the transform's fingerprint of the normal, and uniqueness
(@thm-cf-uniqueness) is what licenses reading the distribution off it. This single sketch
is the whole reason the MGF/CF machinery sits exactly where it does in the track.

# When to reach for these transforms — and when not {#sec-applicability}

An honest applicability verdict, the kind an interviewer is really testing for.

**Reach for the MGF / characteristic function when:**

- You need the **distribution of a sum** of independent variables. Multiply transforms,
  recognize the product, invoke uniqueness. This is the single most common use and the one
  that pays the most.
- You need **moments** of a distribution whose density is awkward to integrate against
  $x^k$ repeatedly, but whose MGF is a clean closed form (gamma, normal, Poisson). Two
  derivatives beat two integrals.
- You are proving a **limit theorem** (CLT-type). The characteristic function plus the
  continuity theorem is the standard machine, because convergence of $\varphi$ is exactly
  convergence in distribution.
- You want an **exponential tail bound** (Chernoff). The MGF, where it exists, gives
  bounds far sharper than Chebyshev's $1/k^2$ — see [PROB-19] for the second-moment version
  this strengthens.

**Do not reach for them when:**

- The variables you are summing are **dependent**. The product rule (@thm-conv) used
  independence in @lem-product; for dependent sums the MGF of the sum is *not* the product,
  and the whole convenience evaporates. You would need the joint MGF, which is rarely
  tractable.
- You only need the mean and variance and the density is already simple — computing $M_X$
  is overkill; just integrate $x f(x)$ and $x^2 f(x)$ directly.
- The distribution is **heavy-tailed** and you specifically want the MGF — it does not
  exist. Use the characteristic function, or work with the distribution directly.
- You need an **exact small-$n$ answer for a non-closed family** (e.g. the exact
  distribution of a sum of a handful of uniforms): the transform exists but inverting it
  back to a density is its own hard problem; direct convolution or simulation may be more
  honest.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The product rule is an independence rule, not an addition rule.** The seductive error is
to write $M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t)M_Y(t)$ for *any* $X+Y$. It holds **only when $X$ and $Y$ are
independent** — that is the single hypothesis @lem-product needs. If $X$ and $Y$ are
correlated, the MGF of the sum involves their joint distribution and does not factor. (By
contrast, the *means* always add, $E[X+Y]=E[X]+E[Y]$, independent or not — that is
linearity of expectation from [PROB-05], a strictly weaker and unconditional statement. Do
not confuse "means always add" with "transforms always multiply.")
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the concentration side of Movement 4. **[PROB-19 *Chebyshev's
Inequality*]** controlled the tails using only the mean and variance — the first two
moments. This article built the object that holds *all* the moments at once and that turns
sums into products. From here the track converges:

- **[PROB-21 *Modes of Convergence*]** — the immediate sequel. The characteristic function
  defined here is the tool that makes **convergence in distribution** precise (the
  continuity theorem) and provides the cleanest uniqueness statement (@thm-cf-uniqueness).
  Everything in @sec-clt-link is set up there in full.
- **[INFR-A2]** (Statistical Inference) — *owns* the **central limit theorem** and the law
  of large numbers. It consumes the MGF/CF machinery built here exactly as sketched in
  @sec-clt-link; the raw transform is ours, the limit theorem is theirs. This is the clean
  ownership seam the track was designed around.
- **[PROB-16 *The Exponential Family*]** — a sibling unification: there, $A'(\theta) =
  E[T(X)]$ generates moments by differentiating the *log-partition*; here $M_X^{(k)}(0) =
  E[X^k]$ generates them by differentiating the MGF. The two devices are close cousins (the
  MGF is essentially the exponential-family normalizer evaluated along $t$), and seeing both
  is worth the trip.
- **Backward links honored.** The catalog MGFs of @sec-catalog certify, in one line each,
  the closure facts that [PROB-11], [PROB-12], [PROB-13], and [PROB-14] could only assert —
  closing the loop on "a sum of independent normals is normal," and its siblings.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary thread.** Laplace's *Théorie analytique des probabilités* (1812) used
  generating functions and the Laplace transform on sums of random quantities and the first
  central-limit arguments — the historical root of @sec-why-history. Paul Lévy's *Calcul des
  probabilités* (1925) put the characteristic function at the center and proved the
  continuity theorem that links it to convergence in distribution.
- **The moment problem.** The fact that an MGF finite on an interval pins down the
  distribution, while the moment *sequence* alone need not (the pitfall in @sec-uniqueness),
  is the classical Hamburger/Stieltjes moment problem. The standard counterexample — two
  distinct distributions with identical moments — is the log-normal and its sine-tilted
  cousins; chasing it down is a rewarding rabbit hole.
- **Cumulants.** Taking $\log M_X(t)$ instead of $M_X(t)$ gives the **cumulant generating
  function**, whose derivatives at $0$ are the *cumulants* — the first is the mean, the
  second the variance, the third relates to skewness — and which *add* (rather than
  multiply) under independent sums. For the normal, $\log M_X(t) = \mu t +
  \tfrac{\sigma^2}{2}t^2$ has only two nonzero cumulants, a clean characterization of "the
  normal is the distribution with no structure beyond mean and variance."
- **Open threads deferred.** *Exactly when is a moment sequence a valid one?* (the moment
  problem's positivity conditions). *How does the characteristic function detect the speed
  of convergence in the CLT?* (the Berry–Esseen theorem, downstream in Inference). *What is
  the multivariate version?* (the joint MGF $E[e^{\mathbf t^\top \mathbf X}]$ of a random
  vector, which combines naturally with the covariance machinery of [PROB-17] and
  [PROB-18]).
